cat1_aortic_dissection.text:
CLINICAL INDICATION: 58-year-old male presenting with sudden onset tearing chest pain radiating to the back, hypertension.

TECHNIQUE: CT angiography of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis was performed with intravenous contrast in the arterial and venous phases.

FINDINGS:
AORTA: There is an intimal flap originating at the level of the aortic root, 1.5 cm above the sinotubular junction, extending inferiorly through the descending thoracic aorta to the level of the celiac axis. The true lumen is compressed by the false lumen, which is larger in caliber (true lumen diameter 2.1 cm, false lumen 3.8 cm). The false lumen demonstrates delayed enhancement consistent with slower flow.

The coronary ostia arise from the true lumen. The aortic arch vessels, including the innominate artery, left common carotid artery, and left subclavian artery, arise from the true lumen without evidence of compromise.

The celiac axis and superior mesenteric artery arise from the true lumen without compromise. The bilateral renal arteries arise from the true lumen.

PERICARDIUM: Mild pericardial effusion is present, measuring up to 8 mm in thickness. No frank tamponade physiology on this static examination.

HEART: Mildly enlarged cardiac silhouette. No other intracardiac abnormality identified.

LUNGS: Small bilateral pleural effusions, right greater than left. No parenchymal consolidation.

IMPRESSION:
1. TYPE A AORTIC DISSECTION — intimal flap originating above the sinotubular junction extending to the celiac axis level. This is a surgical emergency.
2. Mild pericardial effusion without imaging evidence of tamponade.
3. Small bilateral pleural effusions, likely reactive.

ATTENDING RADIOLOGIST NOTATION: I have called the ordering physician, Dr. Chen, directly at 14:32 to communicate this critical finding. Patient requires emergent cardiothoracic surgical consultation.

cat1_tension_pneumothorax.text:
CLINICAL INDICATION: 34-year-old male, status post motor vehicle collision, decreased breath sounds on the left.

TECHNIQUE: Single AP portable chest radiograph.

FINDINGS:
There is complete opacification of the left hemithorax with absence of pulmonary markings throughout. A sharp visceral pleural line is visible at the left apex. The mediastinum is displaced to the right, with the trachea deviated approximately 2.5 cm to the right of midline. The right lung appears hyperinflated and is compressed against the right chest wall. The left hemidiaphragm is depressed and inverted.

No rib fractures are identified on this examination, though evaluation is limited on this portable AP view.

Endotracheal tube tip is at the level of the carina — recommend withdrawal by 2 cm.

IMPRESSION:
1. LARGE LEFT TENSION PNEUMOTHORAX with contralateral mediastinal shift and tracheal deviation. Immediate needle decompression followed by chest tube placement is required.
2. Endotracheal tube tip at the carina — recommend withdrawal.

ATTENDING RADIOLOGIST NOTATION: Trauma team notified via direct phone call at 09:14. This is a critical, life-threatening finding.

cat1_intracranial_hemorrhage.text:
CLINICAL INDICATION: 72-year-old female on warfarin presenting with sudden onset severe headache, worst of her life, GCS 13.

TECHNIQUE: Non-contrast CT of the brain.

FINDINGS:
BRAIN PARENCHYMA: There is a large hyperdense collection within the right basal ganglia measuring 4.2 x 3.8 x 3.5 cm (volume approximately 29 mL by ABC/2 method), consistent with acute intraparenchymal hemorrhage. Surrounding vasogenic edema extends into the right temporal and frontal white matter. There is mass effect on the right lateral ventricle with effacement of the right temporal horn.

VENTRICLES: Hyperdense material is present within the right lateral ventricle and the third ventricle, consistent with intraventricular extension of hemorrhage. The temporal horns of both lateral ventricles are mildly dilated, raising concern for early obstructive hydrocephalus.

MIDLINE: The midline is shifted 6 mm to the left.

BASAL CISTERNS: Mildly compressed but patent.

No extra-axial collections. No evidence of herniation on this examination.

IMPRESSION:
1. LARGE RIGHT BASAL GANGLIA HYPERTENSIVE HEMORRHAGE (approximately 29 mL) with intraventricular extension, 6 mm midline shift, and early obstructive hydrocephalus. This is a critical finding.
2. Neurosurgery and neurology should be consulted emergently.

ATTENDING RADIOLOGIST NOTATION: Emergency department physician and neurosurgery on-call were notified by phone at 22:47.

cat2_pulmonary_embolism.text:
CLINICAL INDICATION: 48-year-old female with pleuritic chest pain and elevated D-dimer, recent long-haul flight.

TECHNIQUE: CT pulmonary angiography with intravenous contrast.

FINDINGS:
PULMONARY ARTERIES: There are filling defects within the right lower lobe segmental and subsegmental pulmonary arteries consistent with acute pulmonary emboli. The right lower lobe posterior basal segmental artery shows near-complete occlusion. Multiple subsegmental branches in the right lower lobe are also involved.

The main, right, and left main pulmonary arteries are patent. The right heart is not enlarged. No saddle embolus.

LUNGS: A peripheral wedge-shaped opacity is present in the right lower lobe posteriorly, which may represent a pulmonary infarct. No other consolidation. No pleural effusion.

HEART: The right ventricle to left ventricle ratio measures 0.9 (within normal limits). No septal bowing. No pericardial effusion.

IMPRESSION:
1. Acute pulmonary emboli involving segmental and subsegmental branches of the right lower lobe pulmonary artery. The central pulmonary arteries are patent; this is a non-massive PE without evidence of right heart strain.
2. Possible right lower lobe pulmonary infarct.
3. Anticoagulation should be initiated. Recommend clinical correlation with hemodynamic status.

ATTENDING RADIOLOGIST NOTATION: Results communicated to ordering provider by phone within the urgent timeframe per ACR guidelines.

cat2_lung_mass.text:
CLINICAL INDICATION: 67-year-old male with 40 pack-year smoking history presenting with hemoptysis and 10 lb unintentional weight loss over 2 months.

TECHNIQUE: CT of the chest with intravenous contrast.

FINDINGS:
LUNGS: There is a 3.4 x 2.9 cm spiculated mass in the right upper lobe posteriorly abutting the major fissure. The mass demonstrates irregular, spiculated margins and pleural tethering. No satellite nodules. There is a 1.1 cm enlarged right paratracheal lymph node (station 4R) and a 0.9 cm subcarinal lymph node (station 7).

No additional pulmonary nodules. No pleural effusion. No consolidation.

MEDIASTINUM: Right paratracheal and subcarinal adenopathy as described above. No other mediastinal masses.

ABDOMEN (limited coverage): No adrenal masses identified on the limited views obtained.

IMPRESSION:
1. RIGHT UPPER LOBE SPICULATED MASS (3.4 cm) — morphology and clinical context are highly suspicious for primary lung malignancy. Staging CT of the abdomen/pelvis and PET-CT are recommended.
2. Right paratracheal and subcarinal lymphadenopathy — possible N2 disease.
3. Tissue sampling (CT-guided biopsy or bronchoscopy) required for histological confirmation.
4. Multidisciplinary thoracic oncology tumor board referral recommended.

ATTENDING RADIOLOGIST NOTATION: Finding communicated to the referring physician as an urgent result per ACR Cat 2 guidelines.

cat2_appendicitis.text:
CLINICAL INDICATION: 22-year-old female with right lower quadrant pain, nausea, and low-grade fever.

TECHNIQUE: CT of the abdomen and pelvis with intravenous and oral contrast.

FINDINGS:
APPENDIX: The appendix is visualized in the right lower quadrant and is dilated, measuring 10 mm in maximal diameter (normal <6 mm). The appendiceal wall is thickened with avid mural enhancement. There is periappendiceal fat stranding and a small amount of free fluid adjacent to the appendiceal tip. No appendicolith identified. No drainable periappendiceal abscess. No perforation identified on this examination, though this cannot be entirely excluded.

BOWEL: No small bowel obstruction. No other bowel wall thickening.

OTHER SOLID ORGANS: Liver, spleen, pancreas, kidneys, and adrenal glands are unremarkable.

GYNECOLOGIC STRUCTURES: The uterus and bilateral ovaries are visualized and normal in appearance. No adnexal pathology.

IMPRESSION:
1. ACUTE APPENDICITIS — dilated, thick-walled appendix with periappendiceal inflammatory changes. No definitive perforation or abscess identified, though early perforation cannot be excluded given the degree of inflammation.
2. Surgical consultation recommended promptly.

ATTENDING RADIOLOGIST NOTATION: Results communicated to the emergency department physician by phone.

cat3_degenerative_spine.text:
CLINICAL INDICATION: 55-year-old male with chronic lower back pain.

TECHNIQUE: MRI of the lumbar spine performed without contrast in sagittal and axial planes.

FINDINGS:
VERTEBRAL BODIES: Vertebral body heights are maintained throughout. Modic type II end plate changes are noted at L4-5 and L5-S1, consistent with fatty degeneration. No acute fracture or compression.

DISCS: Multilevel degenerative disc disease with loss of disc signal and height, most pronounced at L4-5 and L5-S1.

L3-4: Mild disc bulge with mild bilateral facet hypertrophy. No significant canal stenosis. Mild bilateral neural foraminal narrowing.

L4-5: Broad-based disc bulge with superimposed left paracentral protrusion indenting the ventral thecal sac. Left L5 nerve root is mildly compressed within the foramen. Moderate bilateral facet arthropathy. Mild to moderate central canal stenosis (canal AP diameter 10 mm). Moderate left and mild right foraminal stenosis.

L5-S1: Posterior disc bulge with mild bilateral facet hypertrophy. No significant canal stenosis. Mild bilateral foraminal narrowing.

CONUS MEDULLARIS: Normal position and signal, terminating at L1.

IMPRESSION:
1. Multilevel degenerative disc disease and facet arthropathy, most significant at L4-5 with mild to moderate central canal stenosis and moderate left foraminal stenosis with mild left L5 nerve root compression.
2. Clinical correlation recommended. Consider physical therapy, pain management, or surgical consultation if conservative measures fail.

This is a routine finding communicated through standard reporting channels.

cat3_incidental_cyst.text:
CLINICAL INDICATION: 44-year-old female with abdominal pain, rule out appendicitis.

TECHNIQUE: CT abdomen and pelvis with intravenous and oral contrast.

FINDINGS:
APPENDIX: The appendix is visualized in the right lower quadrant, measures 5 mm in diameter, and is normal in appearance. No periappendiceal inflammatory change. No appendicolith.

LIVER: A 2.3 cm well-circumscribed homogeneous low-attenuation lesion is present in hepatic segment VI. The lesion demonstrates peripheral nodular enhancement on the arterial phase with centripetal fill-in on delayed imaging, characteristic of a benign hepatic hemangioma. No other hepatic lesion.

KIDNEYS: A 1.8 cm simple-appearing cyst is present in the lower pole of the left kidney (Bosniak category I — no internal septations, wall thickening, or calcification). No solid renal lesion. Normal bilateral renal enhancement.

OTHER SOLID ORGANS: Spleen, pancreas, and adrenal glands are unremarkable. No adenopathy. No free fluid.

GYNECOLOGIC STRUCTURES: Small amount of free fluid in the cul-de-sac, physiologic. Uterus and ovaries are normal.

IMPRESSION:
1. No appendicitis.
2. Incidental hepatic hemangioma (2.3 cm, segment VI) — benign, no follow-up required.
3. Incidental simple left renal cyst (Bosniak I, 1.8 cm) — benign, no follow-up required per ACR guidelines.

Results available through the standard reporting workflow.

none_normal_cxr.text:
CLINICAL INDICATION: 30-year-old female, pre-employment screening chest radiograph.

TECHNIQUE: PA and lateral chest radiographs.

FINDINGS:
LUNGS: The lungs are clear bilaterally. No consolidation, pleural effusion, pneumothorax, or pulmonary vascular congestion. Lung volumes are normal.

HEART: Cardiac silhouette is normal in size and configuration. Cardiothoracic ratio is 0.42.

MEDIASTINUM: The mediastinum is normal in width. The aortic knuckle and tracheal air column are midline. No hilar adenopathy or mass.

BONES: Visualized osseous structures are intact. No acute fracture or aggressive bony lesion.

SOFT TISSUES: Unremarkable.

IMPRESSION:
1. Normal chest radiograph. No acute cardiopulmonary process.

none_stable_followup.text:
CLINICAL INDICATION: 61-year-old male with history of remote right upper lobe pulmonary nodule, 6-month follow-up.

TECHNIQUE: Low-dose CT of the chest without contrast, performed per Lung-RADS protocol.

FINDINGS:
PULMONARY NODULES: The previously identified right upper lobe solid pulmonary nodule (series 3, image 24) measures 6 mm in greatest dimension, unchanged from the prior CT dated 6 months ago (6 mm). No new nodules identified above the 6 mm threshold.

A 4 mm ground-glass nodule in the left lower lobe is stable compared to prior examination.

LUNGS: No consolidation, pleural effusion, or pneumothorax. Mild centrilobular emphysema in the bilateral upper lobes, consistent with smoking history.

MEDIASTINUM: No adenopathy. No mass.

HEART: No pericardial effusion. Mild coronary artery calcification.

IMPRESSION:
1. Stable 6 mm right upper lobe solid nodule — Lung-RADS 3. Recommend continued annual low-dose CT surveillance.
2. Stable left lower lobe ground-glass nodule — Lung-RADS 2.
3. Mild centrilobular emphysema.

No critical finding. Results available through the standard radiology workflow.
